reviews.summary: The paper proposes Attention Entropy Profiling (AEP), a training-free OOD detector for ViTs that builds a per-layer profile from attention entropy, concentration, and head-agreement statistics and scores samples by Mahalanobis distance to an ID calibration set. The paper evaluates AEP and a fused variant against MSP, Energy, ViM, and KNN on Food-101 as ID and five OOD datasets across three ImageNet-pretrained ViTs, with ablations on components, layers, calibration size, and overhead.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty search and did not find this exact title indexed, but I did find closely related prior work that weakens the paper's main novelty claim. Most importantly, ICCVW 2023 already proposed ViT-attention-based OOD detection in "Leveraging Visual Attention for out-of-Distribution Detection" (CVF Open Access: https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/OODCV/html/Cultrera_Leveraging_Visual_Attention_for_out-of-Distribution_Detection_ICCVW_2023_paper.html), using ViT attention heatmaps as the detection signal. I also found "PViT: Prior-augmented Vision Transformer for Out-of-distribution Detection" (arXiv:2410.20631, https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.20631), which is a recent ViT-specific OOD method, and older ViT OOD work such as OODFormer. On the reference side, the paper's more peripheral citations such as CalAttn (arXiv:2508.08547), TCA/"Is Less More?" (arXiv:2410.14729, later ICCV 2025), and "Attention Head Entropy of LLMs Predicts Answer Correctness" (arXiv:2602.13699) do appear to exist. Overall, the contribution looks like a specific training-free entropy-profile variant within an already explored direction, not the 'first systematic use' of ViT attention for OOD detection; novelty is therefore limited.
strengths: The method is simple, interpretable, and easy to implement: the core AEP computation in the provided code matches the paper's high-level description of extracting CLS entropy, average token entropy, concentration, and head agreement from attention maps.

The paper is generally well written and unusually candid about major limitations; the Discussion section explicitly acknowledges the non-standard Food-101 setup, missing calibration validation, and implementation overhead.
The workspace contains substantial supporting material: experiment code, aggregated results, figure-generation scripts, and experiment logs, which makes auditing possible.
weaknesses: Automatic-rejection integrity issue: the manuscript tables do not exactly match results.json. Examples include DeiT-S AEP+Fusion on Flowers-102 (paper 99.7 vs results 99.6 AUROC), DeiT-B AEP on Textures (98.4 vs 98.3), ViT-B KNN on CIFAR-100 (100.0 vs 99.9), ViT-B AEP on Flowers-102 (89.6 vs 89.5), and multiple statistical-test table mismatches.

Automatic-rejection soundness issue: the fusion code does not implement the paper's description. In the provided code, beta is selected by maximizing AUROC on the actual ID/OOD evaluation scores, and even the baseline to fuse with is chosen using test OOD performance. This is test-set leakage and materially inflates the fusion results.

The evaluation protocol is not scientifically persuasive for OOD detection. Using Food-101 as ID with ImageNet-pretrained models means the classifier label space is mismatched to the ID task; the paper itself notes zero classification accuracy, which makes both practical relevance and any calibration claims largely vacuous.

Three of the five OOD datasets are 32x32 datasets resized to 224x224, creating an obvious resolution cue and severe ceiling effects. As a result, the strongest baselines already achieve nearly perfect AUROC, so the experiments do not convincingly isolate the value of AEP.
The novelty claim is overstated because prior work already uses ViT attention as an OOD signal; the paper's real novelty is narrower than claimed.
reviews.detailed_feedback: To make this publishable, the authors need a clean re-evaluation rather than a light revision. First, fix the integrity problems: regenerate every table directly from the saved result files and reconcile all mismatches. Second, remove test leakage from fusion; beta and any method-selection logic must be tuned without access to evaluation OOD labels. Third, rerun the study on a standard benchmark with a matched ID task and realistic OOD sets, ideally ImageNet/OpenOOD-style evaluation, and avoid low-resolution artifacts dominating the task. Fourth, either drop the calibration contribution or evaluate it in a setting where the model has meaningful accuracy. Finally, reposition the contribution honestly relative to prior attention-based ViT OOD work: the claim should be about a training-free entropy-profile formulation, not about being the first to use attention for ViT OOD detection.
questions_for_authors: Why does the released fusion code tune beta by maximizing AUROC on the actual ID/OOD evaluation scores, rather than on a held-out calibration procedure as stated in the paper?
Can you provide a corrected manuscript in which every table entry is generated directly from results.json and all current mismatches are removed?
Can you rerun the method on a benchmark where the model has non-trivial ID accuracy and the OOD sets do not differ trivially in native resolution?
What is the intended novelty claim relative to ICCVW 2023 'Leveraging Visual Attention for Out-of-Distribution Detection' and other ViT-specific OOD works such as PViT?
reviews.integrity_check: Source code is present and the experimental pipeline did run; exp/results/experiment_log.txt shows full extraction/evaluation jobs, and the figure scripts read from the saved experiment outputs. However, the release fails the paper-integrity check because the manuscript contains multiple numeric mismatches against results.json, and the fusion implementation leaks evaluation OOD labels by tuning beta on test scores rather than a proper calibration split.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Attention Entropy Profiling (AEP), a training-free OOD detection method for Vision Transformers that constructs a compact profile from four layerwise attention statistics (CLS attention entropy, average token entropy, concentration ratio, head agreement) and scores inputs via Mahalanobis distance from in-distribution reference statistics. Experiments on three ViT architectures with Food-101 as the in-distribution dataset show AUROC 0.92-1.00, with fusion of AEP and KNN achieving the best performance. The paper honestly acknowledges a critical limitation: the pretrained models have zero classification accuracy on the ID dataset, making this a non-standard evaluation.
reviews.novelty_assessment: Online search reveals the specific 4-statistic profile + Mahalanobis combination has not been published before. However, the core insight — that attention entropy in ViTs encodes distributional information — has strong precedent: (1) AttEntropy (Atzeni et al., 2022, arXiv:2212.14397) uses spatial attention entropy from transformers to segment OOD objects; (2) AttenDence/LookSharp (Mali & Shelhamer, 2025, arXiv:2511.18925) directly uses CLS-to-patch attention entropy in ViTs for test-time adaptation under distribution shift — this highly relevant work is NOT cited; (3) The paper's own cited reference Ostmeier et al. (2026) shows attention head entropy predicts LLM behavior, making AEP essentially an application of this insight to ViT OOD detection; (4) Entropy-Lens (2025, arXiv:2502.16570) demonstrates layerwise entropy profiles as informative signatures of transformer computation. The Mahalanobis scoring component is borrowed directly from Lee et al. (2018). The novelty is incremental: a reasonable but somewhat straightforward combination of known techniques.
strengths: Exceptionally honest self-assessment: the paper clearly flags that the ID dataset (Food-101) yields zero classification accuracy with ImageNet-pretrained models, that 3/5 OOD datasets have resolution artifacts making detection trivial, and that the calibration evaluation was not completed. This level of transparency is commendable.

Thorough ablation studies: component ablation, layer ablation, and calibration set size sensitivity provide genuine insight into which parts of the method contribute and why. CLS entropy being most important is well-supported by data.

Sound statistical framework: Hotelling's T² tests with Bonferroni correction provide formal evidence that attention entropy profiles differ between ID and OOD. Multiple seeds (3) with reported standard deviations add credibility.

Clean method formulation: the 4-statistic entropy profile is well-motivated, clearly defined mathematically (Equations 1-6), and the implementation matches the description. The code is well-structured and readable.
The fusion approach (AEP+KNN) consistently outperforms individual methods, empirically demonstrating that attention-level and feature-level signals are complementary.
weaknesses: Critical evaluation flaw: Using Food-101 as the ID dataset with models that have ZERO classification accuracy fundamentally undermines the evaluation. This measures whether attention patterns differ between dataset domains for non-task-adapted models, NOT whether AEP provides useful OOD detection in the practical regime. Standard OOD benchmarks (OpenOOD with ImageNet-1K as ID) are essential and were not conducted.

Ceiling effects on 3/5 OOD datasets: SVHN, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 are 32×32 images resized to 224×224, making detection trivially easy for ALL methods (AUROC > 0.98). This means meaningful comparison is possible on only 2 of 5 datasets (Textures and Flowers-102), where AEP underperforms KNN — especially on Flowers-102 where AEP's FPR95 reaches 19-45% vs KNN's 1-3%.

AEP does NOT outperform existing baselines standalone: KNN consistently beats AEP on AUROC and FPR95 across all models and datasets. The paper's value proposition rests on fusion (AEP+KNN), but this introduces additional complexity (tuning β) while the gains over KNN alone are marginal on the informative benchmarks.

Missing key related work: AttenDence/LookSharp (Mali & Shelhamer, 2025) uses CLS attention entropy in ViTs for distribution shift adaptation — directly demonstrating awareness of the same core signal. Not citing this work undermines the claim of being 'the first systematic use of layerwise attention entropy' as a distribution shift signal in ViTs.

Significant computational overhead: 7-11× inference slowdown is a major practical barrier. The paper attributes this to monkey-patching but does not demonstrate the claimed near-zero overhead with native attention output, making this an unsubstantiated claim.

Incomplete contributions: The input-adaptive calibration component (Section 3.5) is proposed but never evaluated. This weakens the paper's contribution list since one of the claimed contributions is absent from the experimental validation.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The core idea — using attention entropy patterns for OOD detection — is reasonable and worth investigating, but the paper falls short in execution. The most critical action for revision is re-running all experiments with ImageNet-1K as the in-distribution dataset using the OpenOOD protocol (SSB-hard, NINCO for near-OOD; iNaturalist, Textures, OpenImage-O for far-OOD). This would (1) enable direct comparison with published methods, (2) test AEP in the practical regime where models have non-trivial accuracy, and (3) eliminate the resolution artifact confound. Replace SVHN/CIFAR-10/CIFAR-100 with standard near-OOD benchmarks that share resolution with ImageNet. Second, cite and discuss AttenDence (arXiv:2511.18925), AttEntropy (arXiv:2212.14397), and Entropy-Lens (arXiv:2502.16570) — position AEP clearly relative to these works. Third, either implement and evaluate the calibration component or remove it from the contributions. Fourth, demonstrate inference overhead with native attention output support in a modern framework to validate the efficiency claims. Finally, add stronger baselines such as ASH (which is mentioned in related work but not evaluated), DICE, and ReAct to better position AEP's contribution.
questions_for_authors: Have you attempted running on ImageNet-1K as ID with the OpenOOD protocol? What were the results, or what prevented this evaluation?
On the two informative benchmarks (Textures, Flowers-102), AEP underperforms KNN standalone. What is the practical argument for using AEP when KNN is simpler and more effective?
How does AEP perform on near-OOD detection (e.g., SSB-hard, NINCO) where the distribution shift is subtle? The current far-OOD benchmarks may overstate AEP's utility.
Are you aware of AttenDence (Mali & Shelhamer, 2025) which uses CLS attention entropy for test-time adaptation in ViTs? How does AEP relate to and differ from that work?

The fusion weight β ≈ 0.8 heavily favors AEP over KNN. Is this because the Mahalanobis distance score distribution happens to have better calibration, or because AEP genuinely provides complementary information?
reviews.integrity_check: Results integrity is strong. All numbers in Tables 1-2 (AUROC and FPR95) were verified against results.json and match to the reported precision. Ablation numbers (Table 3) also match results.json exactly. Experiment source code exists and implements the described method: aep.py correctly implements CLS entropy, average token entropy, concentration ratio, head agreement, and Mahalanobis scoring as formulated in the paper. Baselines (MSP, Energy, ViM, KNN) are implemented in baselines.py. Multiple experiment logs exist showing actual execution. Figures are generated from code (generate_figures.py, generate_figures_v2.py). All 21 cited references were verified as real publications. No evidence of fabrication or data manipulation.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Attention Entropy Profiling (AEP), a training-free method for detecting out-of-distribution inputs in Vision Transformers by analyzing layerwise self-attention statistics. The method constructs a profile vector from four per-layer statistics (CLS attention entropy, average token entropy, attention concentration ratio, and head agreement) and scores test inputs using Mahalanobis distance from in-distribution reference statistics. The authors demonstrate competitive AUROC performance (0.92-1.00) across three ViT architectures and five OOD datasets, with fusion to feature-based detectors yielding the best results.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted extensive online searches to verify novelty claims. The key related works found are: (1) OODformer (BMVC 2021) - uses ViTs for OOD detection but relies on CLS token embeddings and class-conditioned similarity, not layerwise attention entropy statistics; (2) Leveraging Visual Attention for OOD Detection (ICCVW 2023) - trains an autoencoder on attention heatmaps for reconstruction-based detection, fundamentally different from using entropy statistics directly; (3) Head Entropy (Ostmeier et al., arXiv 2026) - uses attention entropy in LLMs for correctness prediction, not OOD detection in vision. The specific combination of layerwise attention entropy profiling with the four statistics (CLS entropy, average token entropy, concentration ratio, head agreement) combined with Mahalanobis distance scoring appears to be a novel contribution. However, the paper's claim of being 'the first systematic use of layerwise attention entropy profiles for OOD detection in Vision Transformers' should be nuanced to acknowledge these related works.
strengths: The proposed AEP method is training-free and requires no architectural modifications, making it practical for deployment.
The paper presents comprehensive ablation studies showing CLS attention entropy is the most informative component and late layers are most discriminative.
Statistical validation (Hotelling's T² tests, p < 10⁻¹⁶) provides strong evidence that attention entropy profiles differ between ID and OOD inputs.
The fusion approach (AEP+KNN) consistently outperforms individual methods, demonstrating that attention-level and feature-level signals are complementary.
The paper honestly acknowledges its major limitations in a dedicated section, including the non-standard evaluation protocol.
The code and experimental logs are present, enabling verification of results.
weaknesses: CRITICAL: The evaluation uses Food-101 as ID with ImageNet-pretrained models that have ZERO classification accuracy on Food-101. This is a highly non-standard protocol that limits practical relevance and comparability to published benchmarks.
CRITICAL: Three of five OOD datasets (SVHN, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100) are 32×32 images resized to 224×224, creating resolution artifacts that likely trivialize detection regardless of method.
Missing calibration evaluation on appropriate benchmarks (ImageNet-C, ImageNet-R) - the proposed input-adaptive temperature scaling component was not properly evaluated.
Swin Transformer was planned but not evaluated, despite being listed in the experimental scope.
The claim that AEP 'is the first to exploit layerwise attention statistics for OOD detection in ViTs' (Section 2) should be qualified given OODformer (BMVC 2021) and Cultrera et al. (ICCVW 2023).
No error bars or confidence intervals in the main results tables - only std across 3 seeds is reported.
No statistical significance tests for performance comparisons between methods.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper presents an interesting approach to OOD detection using attention entropy profiles. To improve the paper for acceptance, the authors should: (1) Re-run experiments with ImageNet-1K as the ID dataset following standard OpenOOD protocols to enable meaningful comparison with published results. This is essential because the current evaluation measures attention pattern differences for models with zero task accuracy. (2) Replace low-resolution OOD datasets (SVHN, CIFAR-10/100) with standard near-OOD datasets like SSB-hard, NINCO, iNaturalist, and OpenImage-O. (3) Complete the calibration evaluation on ImageNet-C/R corruptions as proposed. (4) Include Swin Transformer results or remove it from claims. (5) Add discussion comparing AEP to OODformer (BMVC 2021) and Cultrera et al. (ICCVW 2023) to clarify the differences. The paper is well-written and the method is sound, but the experimental setup must be addressed to ensure the results are meaningful and comparable to the literature.
questions_for_authors: How does AEP differ fundamentally from OODformer (BMVC 2021), which also uses ViTs for OOD detection?
Can you re-run experiments with ImageNet-1K as ID following OpenOOD protocols to enable fair comparison with published methods?
Why were low-resolution datasets (SVHN, CIFAR) used when they create resolution artifacts that trivialize detection?
What is the actual runtime overhead when using native attention output support (e.g., with output_attentions=True) rather than monkey-patching?
Was the proposed input-adaptive temperature calibration evaluated on ImageNet-C/R, and if not, why is it included as a contribution?
reviews.integrity_check: The experiment code exists in exp/ directory and appears to implement the described method. The results.json file contains numerical values that match the paper's tables (e.g., DeiT-S AEP AUROC on Textures: 0.9877 in results.json vs 98.8% in Table 1). The experiment logs show actual training runs with progress bars and timestamps. The references checked (Ostmeier et al. arXiv:2602.13699, Liang et al. arXiv:2508.08547, Wang et al. ICCV 2025) all exist with the stated titles and authors. However, the non-standard evaluation setup (Food-101 ID with 0% accuracy models) significantly limits the validity and comparability of the results.
